walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Bunny Kingdom set up. First, place the game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The board shows a ten by ten grid of territories with coordinates along the edges. Columns are labeled A through J and rows are numbered one through ten.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find all the building tokens and sort them into separate supply piles near the board. You will have city tokens in three levels, which show one, two, or three towers. You will also have farm tokens and sky tower tokens. Keep these organized so players can grab them quickly during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Resource Tokens
walkthrough.text: Some territories on the board already show a printed resource icon. Place the matching basic resource tokens on those territories. There are five resource types in the game: wood, fish, carrots, mushrooms, and gems. Keep any extra resource tokens in a supply pile nearby.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Colors
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes all thirty-six rabbit figurines of that color. These are the little bunny figures you will place on the board to claim territories. Set them in a pile near your play area where you can grab them easily.

walkthrough.title: Set Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player places their score marker on zero on the score track that runs around the edge of the board. You will be scoring at the end of every round, so your marker will move throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal the Exploration Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the large exploration deck of one hundred eighty-two cards. Give it a thorough shuffle. In a four-player game, deal ten cards to each player. In a three-player game, deal twelve cards each. In a two-player game, deal ten cards each.

walkthrough.title: Review Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Go ahead and look at your cards, but keep them hidden from other players. You will see territory cards with grid coordinates, building cards for cities and other structures, provision cards that add resources, and parchment cards with secret objectives. Do not play any cards yet. The game is played over four rounds of drafting and scoring, and we will walk through how that works in the rules.

summary.title: Review Your Hand

Card types: territory, building, provision, parchment
Game plays over 4 rounds of drafting → scoring

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Bunny Kingdom is played over four rounds. In each round, you will draft cards from your hand, play them to claim territories and build on the board, then score your fiefdoms. After the fourth round, you also reveal secret parchment cards for bonus points.

walkthrough.title: How Card Drafting Works
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, you have a hand of cards. Choose two cards from your hand and place them face down in front of you. Then pass your remaining cards to the next player. In rounds one and three you pass left, and in rounds two and four you pass right. Pick up the cards passed to you and repeat until all cards have been drafted.

walkthrough.title: Playing Your Drafted Cards
walkthrough.text: Once all cards are drafted, everyone reveals and plays their kept cards simultaneously. Territory cards show a grid coordinate like B4 or G7. When you play one, place a rabbit on that matching space on the board. You now control that territory and any resource it produces.

walkthrough.title: Building Cards
walkthrough.text: This let you place city tokens, farm tokens, or sky tower tokens on territories you already control. City tokens come in three levels showing one, two, or three towers. Place the matching token on any territory where you have a rabbit. Cities are essential because a fiefdom without a city scores zero points.

walkthrough.title: Provision and Parchment Cards
walkthrough.text: Provision cards add a resource type to one of your territories that does not already have one. This is the only way to get gems, since no territory has gems printed on it. Parchment cards are secret objectives. Keep them face down until the end of the game when they score bonus points.

walkthrough.title: What Is a Fiefdom
walkthrough.text: A fiefdom is a group of your territories that are connected to each other orthogonally, meaning up, down, left, or right. Diagonal connections do not count. A single isolated territory is also a fiefdom by itself. You might have multiple separate fiefdoms on the board, and each one scores independently.

walkthrough.title: How Fiefdom Scoring Works
walkthrough.text: Each fiefdom scores using a simple formula: Strength times Wealth. Strength equals the total city tower levels in that fiefdom. Wealth equals the number of different resource types produced in that fiefdom. So a fiefdom with a level two city and a level one city producing wood, fish, and carrots would score three times three, which equals nine points.

walkthrough.title: Why Strength and Wealth Both Matter
walkthrough.text: Because the formula is multiplication, both sides matter enormously. A fiefdom with Strength five but Wealth one only scores five points. The same fiefdom with Wealth four would score twenty. Always think about boosting whichever side is lagging behind, because adding one more resource type or one more city level multiplies your entire score.

walkthrough.title: Sky Towers and Camps
walkthrough.text: Sky towers are special buildings that create virtual connections between all territories that have sky towers, even if they are on opposite sides of the board. This can merge distant fiefdoms into one powerhouse. Camps are printed on certain territories and act as level one cities automatically if you control them, so you do not need a city card for those spots.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: After everyone has played their cards, score all of your fiefdoms. Calculate Strength times Wealth for each fiefdom and add the totals to your running score on the track. Then deal a new hand of cards from the exploration deck and begin the next round. Remember to alternate your passing direction.

walkthrough.title: Contested Territories
walkthrough.text: Each territory on the board can only be controlled by one player. If you draft a territory card for a space that is already occupied by another player's rabbit, the card is wasted. Pay attention to the board before you draft so you do not accidentally pick a territory that has already been claimed.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring and Parchments
walkthrough.text: After the fourth round's fiefdom scoring, everyone reveals their parchment cards. Each parchment has a specific condition, like bonus points for controlling territories in a certain row or having fiefdoms with high Wealth. Add your parchment points to your total. The player with the highest combined score across all four rounds plus parchments wins the game.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 4 rounds of draft → play → score
After Round 4, reveal parchment cards for bonus points
Highest total score wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Kingdom
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four example players. Each player will make different choices so you can see how drafting, territory claiming, building, and scoring all work together. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Round One Setup
walkthrough.text: Each player has been dealt ten cards. You will draft two cards at a time, then pass the rest to your left since this is round one. After five drafting steps, everyone will have ten drafted cards to play. Let's see what happens.

walkthrough.title: Player One Drafts Smart
walkthrough.text: Player One looks at their hand and sees two adjacent territory cards, D5 and D6, plus a level one city card. They draft D5 and D6, keeping them face down. Grabbing adjacent territories on the first pick is a great move because connected territories form larger fiefdoms. Player One is already planning a connected kingdom.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Grabs a City Early
walkthrough.text: Player Two drafts a territory card for E3 and a level two city card. Getting a city in the first draft is important because a fiefdom without a city scores zero points no matter how many territories or resources it has. Player Two is setting up to score right away.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring Cities
walkthrough.text: Player Three sees two territory cards that produce wood and fish, so they draft both. The territories look great because they have resources. But Player Three has not picked up a single city card yet, and without a city in the fiefdom, those resources multiply by zero. Resources without cities score nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Secure Cities
walkthrough.text: The formula is Strength times Wealth. If Strength is zero because you have no city, it does not matter how high your Wealth is. The result is always zero. Make sure every fiefdom you are building has at least one city, or you are wasting those territories. Draft city cards alongside territory cards from the very first round.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Drafts a Parchment
walkthrough.text: Player Four notices a parchment card that awards three points for each fiefdom with Wealth three or more. They draft it along with a territory card. Parchments are secret objectives scored at game end and can be worth ten to twenty points if you build around them. Drafting one early gives you a goal to work toward.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Scattered Territories
walkthrough.text: In the second drafting step, Player One gets excited and drafts two territory cards on opposite corners of the board, far from their existing D5 and D6 territories. Now they have three separate single-territory fiefdoms instead of one growing cluster. Scattered territories score much less than connected ones.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Connect Your Territories
walkthrough.text: Two fiefdoms of Strength two and Wealth two each score four points apiece, for a total of eight. One merged fiefdom of Strength four and Wealth four scores sixteen. Connecting territories is not just nice to have. It is the single most impactful thing you can do because the multiplication formula rewards size exponentially.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Adds Resources
walkthrough.text: Player Two drafts a provision card for carrots and a territory adjacent to E3 that produces wood. They are thinking ahead. Their fiefdom will have a level two city for Strength and two different resource types for Wealth two. That is already two times two, which equals four points this round.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: No Resource Diversity
walkthrough.text: Player Three drafts another territory that produces wood. They now have three territories, two producing wood and one producing fish. But duplicate resources do not increase Wealth. Their Wealth is still only two because they only have two distinct resource types. They missed a chance to grab a carrot or mushroom territory instead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Diversify Your Resources
walkthrough.text: Wealth counts distinct resource types, not total resources. Having three wood territories still gives you Wealth one for wood. You want one of each type, not multiples of the same type. When drafting, prioritize territories or provision cards that add a new resource type your fiefdom does not already have.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Dismissing Parchments
walkthrough.text: Player Four passes on a second parchment card in favor of a territory card they do not really need. Parchments can easily be worth ten to twenty points at game end, which is often the difference between winning and losing. Passing on parchments that fit your strategy is a costly mistake that new players make all the time.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Underestimate Parchments
walkthrough.text: Parchment cards are scored only at the end of the game, so new players tend to undervalue them. But a single parchment aligned with your board position can outscore an entire fiefdom. Draft parchments that match what you are already building, and let early parchments guide your drafting strategy for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Playing Cards and Claiming Territories
walkthrough.text: All drafting steps are done. Everyone reveals their ten drafted cards and plays them simultaneously. Place rabbits on every territory card's matching grid space. Place city tokens and other buildings on your controlled territories. Tuck provision cards under territories to add resources. Keep parchments face down in your personal area.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Round One
walkthrough.text: Now we score. Player Two has a fiefdom with a level two city, wood, and carrots. That is Strength two times Wealth two, which equals four points. Player One has D5 and D6 connected but no city there, so that fiefdom scores zero. Player One's isolated territories with a city score a little, but the scattered approach cost them. Player Three has resources but no city, scoring zero.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Deal new cards for round two. This time you pass cards to the right. Look at the board and think about what you need. Can you connect your scattered territories? Do you need a city to start scoring? Is there a provision card that would add a new resource type to your best fiefdom? Your first round taught you what to prioritize.

walkthrough.title: Before You Hop In
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to hate-draft and how to evaluate provision cards.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, scoring rules, and special buildings.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up new players the most. First, building fiefdoms without cities, which means those fiefdoms score zero every round. Second, scattering territories across the board instead of connecting them into larger fiefdoms. Third, hoarding duplicate resource types when only distinct types increase Wealth. Fourth, ignoring parchment cards that can swing the final score by twenty or more points.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Cities Above All Else
walkthrough.text: The number one rule in Bunny Kingdom is that a fiefdom without a city scores nothing. It does not matter if you control ten connected territories with all five resource types. Zero Strength times any Wealth equals zero. Always draft at least enough city cards to cover your major fiefdoms. Getting a level two or three city early is especially valuable because it scores every single round, compounding its value over the game.

walkthrough.title: Build Connected Kingdoms
walkthrough.text: The multiplication formula massively rewards large connected fiefdoms over scattered small ones. When you are drafting territory cards, look at what is adjacent to territories you already control. Adding one territory that bridges two of your fiefdoms can double or triple your score by combining their Strength and Wealth. Always ask yourself whether a territory card connects to your existing kingdom before picking it.

walkthrough.title: Chase Resource Diversity
walkthrough.text: Since Wealth counts only distinct resource types, your goal should be to get one of each. A fiefdom with five different resources has Wealth five, and even a single level one city there scores five points per round. Provision cards are your best friend here because they let you add a resource type to any territory you control. Save your provision cards for the fiefdom where a new resource type creates the biggest Wealth jump.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table and Hate-Draft
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on what your opponents are building. If you see someone growing a fiefdom toward a territory that would connect two of their clusters, consider drafting that territory yourself even if you do not need it. This is called hate-drafting, and in Bunny Kingdom it can be devastating. One well-placed block can keep an opponent's fiefdom split in two for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Value Parchments Highly
walkthrough.text: Parchment cards are scored only at game end, which makes new players dismiss them. Do not fall into that trap. A single well-matched parchment can be worth fifteen or more points, easily outscoring an entire round of fiefdom scoring. Draft parchments early when you can still shape your board to meet their conditions. If you see a parchment that matches what you are already building, grab it immediately.

walkthrough.title: Sky Towers Are Game-Changers
walkthrough.text: Sky towers create virtual connections between all territories that have them, regardless of distance. If you control two separate fiefdoms that each have a sky tower, those fiefdoms merge into one for scoring purposes. This can create massive combined Strength and Wealth that would be impossible through adjacency alone. Whenever you see a sky tower card, think about how it could link your scattered territories.

walkthrough.title: Think in Rounds, Not Just End Game
walkthrough.text: Fiefdoms score every round, not just at the end. A fiefdom scoring six points per round earns twenty-four points over four rounds, which is often more than a big parchment card. This means early cities and early resource diversity are incredibly valuable because they compound over time. Do not save everything for a big round four play. Get your fiefdoms scoring as early as possible and let the points accumulate.

Duplicate resources don't increase Wealth
Ignoring parchments can cost you 15-20+ points

Can merge 2 distant fiefdoms into 1 powerhouse

summary.title: Think in Rounds, Not Just End Game
bullets: Fiefdoms score every round — early scoring compounds
6 pts/round × 4 rounds = 24 pts (often more than a parchment)
Get cities and resources early, let points accumulate

walkthrough.title: The Mega-Fiefdom Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most powerful approach in Bunny Kingdom is building one enormous connected fiefdom with maximum Strength and Wealth. Aim for Strength five or higher by stacking multiple cities, and Wealth five by collecting all five resource types. A single fiefdom scoring five times five equals twenty-five points per round, which adds up to one hundred points over four rounds. Focus all your drafting on territories adjacent to your main cluster and provision cards that fill missing resource types.

walkthrough.title: Building the Mega-Fiefdom
walkthrough.text: Start by claiming a cluster of three or four adjacent territories in round one, ideally near the center of the board where you have more expansion directions. Draft at least one city card in round one so you start scoring immediately. In rounds two and three, expand outward with adjacent territory cards and add provision cards for any resource types you are missing. By round four, you want all five resource types and as many city levels as possible in one connected group.

walkthrough.title: Sky Tower Bridge Strategy
walkthrough.text: Sky towers let you connect fiefdoms that are physically separated on the board. The key insight is that you can build two clusters in different areas, each with complementary resources, then link them with sky towers into one mega-fiefdom. If one cluster has wood and fish while the other has carrots and mushrooms, the merged fiefdom suddenly has Wealth four. This strategy is harder to block because opponents cannot easily see what you are connecting.

walkthrough.title: Hate-Drafting and Denial
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay as much attention to their opponents' boards as their own. When you see an opponent about to connect two fiefdoms with a single territory, draft that territory even if it does nothing for you. Denying a connection that would have given them twenty extra points is worth far more than the marginal territory you would have taken instead. The best hate-drafts are territories that block connections and are also useful for your own position.

walkthrough.title: Parchment-First Drafting
walkthrough.text: Some of the highest-scoring games come from players who draft parchments early and build their entire board around them. If you pick up a parchment in round one that rewards three points per fiefdom with Wealth three or more, you can deliberately create multiple small fiefdoms that each hit Wealth three. This approach treats parchments as your primary scoring engine rather than a bonus, and it can generate thirty or more points from parchments alone.

walkthrough.title: Gem Control Strategy
walkthrough.text: Gems are the rarest resource in Bunny Kingdom because they are not printed on any territory. The only way to get gems is through provision cards. If you can secure a gem provision card and add it to your main fiefdom, you gain a Wealth advantage that no opponent can easily replicate. In a competitive game, controlling gems can be the difference between Wealth four and Wealth five, which with Strength five means five extra points per round.

walkthrough.title: Early Cities, Late Resources
walkthrough.text: Experienced players draft cities in rounds one and two and resources in rounds three and four. Cities score every round they exist, so getting them early maximizes their compounding value. Resources also score every round, but their impact is bigger when you already have high Strength. A Strength one fiefdom gaining one more resource goes from one to two points, but a Strength five fiefdom gaining that same resource goes from twenty to twenty-five points. Time your provision cards for maximum multiplier impact.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Draft for Information
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what cards come back to you in the draft. If territory cards near your fiefdom keep getting passed, opponents may not be competing in your area. If city cards are scarce, other players are prioritizing them heavily. Use this information to adjust your strategy mid-round. The draft is not just about picking cards. It is also about reading what your opponents are doing and adapting your plan accordingly.

summary.title: The Mega-Fiefdom Strategy
bullets: One massive connected fiefdom with S5+ and W5
S5 × W5 = 25 pts/round = 100 pts over 4 rounds
Focus all drafting on adjacent territories + provision cards

summary.title: Building the Mega-Fiefdom
bullets: Round 1: claim 3-4 adjacent territories near center + 1 city
Rounds 2-3: expand outward, add provision for missing resources
Round 4: maximize city levels and fill last resource gaps

summary.title: Sky Tower Bridge Strategy

Harder for opponents to block — connection is virtual

summary.title: Hate-Drafting and Denial

Denying 20 pts of connections > gaining 1 marginal territory
Best hate-drafts also serve your own position

summary.title: Parchment-First Drafting

Multiple small fiefdoms hitting parchment thresholds = huge points
Can generate 30+ VP from parchments alone

summary.title: Gem Control Strategy

With S5: W4→W5 = +5 pts/round = +20 pts over 4 rounds

summary.title: Early Cities, Late Resources
bullets: Cities in Rounds 1-2 = maximum compounding (score every round)
Resources in Rounds 3-4 = bigger multiplier impact on high-Strength fiefdoms
S5 gaining 1 resource: +5 pts/round vs S1 gaining 1 resource: +1 pt/round

summary.title: Reading the Draft for Information
bullets: Cards passed to you reveal opponent priorities
Territory cards near you still circulating = less competition
Scarce city cards = opponents hoarding them — adapt accordingly

entries.definition: A group of your territories connected orthogonally (up, down, left, right). Diagonal connections do not count. Each fiefdom scores independently using the Strength times Wealth formula. A single isolated territory is also a fiefdom.

The total number of city tower levels in a fiefdom. A level one city adds one Strength, a level two city adds two, and a level three city adds three. Multiple cities in one fiefdom add their levels together. A fiefdom with zero Strength scores zero points.

The number of distinct resource types produced in a fiefdom. There are five resource types: wood, fish, carrots, mushrooms, and gems. Duplicate resources do not increase Wealth. Maximum Wealth is five.

Each fiefdom scores Strength multiplied by Wealth at the end of every round. Both sides of the equation matter because multiplication means small increases on either side have a large impact on the total score.

The core mechanic of Bunny Kingdom. Choose two cards from your hand, place them face down, then pass the rest to the next player. Rounds one and three pass left, rounds two and four pass right. Repeat until all cards are drafted.

A card showing a grid coordinate like B4 or G7. When played, place one of your rabbit figurines on the matching space on the board. You now control that territory and any resource it produces. If the territory is already occupied, the card is wasted.

A card that lets you place a building token such as a city, farm, or sky tower on one of your controlled territories. Buildings provide Strength, resources, or special connections depending on their type.
A building card that places a city token of level one, two, or three on your territory. Cities provide Strength to the fiefdom. A fiefdom must have at least one city to score any points.

A card that adds a resource type to one of your controlled territories that does not already have a resource. This is the only way to get gems, since no territory has gems printed on it. Provision cards are key to increasing Wealth.

A secret objective card kept face down until the end of the game. Parchments score bonus points based on specific conditions such as controlling territories in a certain row, having fiefdoms with high Wealth, or controlling a set number of fiefdoms. Can be worth ten to twenty or more points.

At the end of each of the four rounds, every player calculates Strength times Wealth for each of their fiefdoms and adds the total to their running score on the track. Fiefdoms score every round, making early scoring compound over the game.

After the fourth round's fiefdom scoring, all players reveal their parchment cards and score them based on their conditions. Parchment points are added to the running total. The player with the highest combined score wins.

A fiefdom with a level two city and a level one city (Strength three) producing wood, fish, and carrots (Wealth three) scores three times three equals nine points. That fiefdom scores nine points every round it exists in that state.

A special building that creates a virtual connection between all territories on the board that have sky towers, regardless of distance. This can merge physically separated fiefdoms into one for scoring purposes, combining their Strength and Wealth.

A symbol printed on certain territories on the board. Camps act as level one cities automatically if you control that territory, providing one Strength without needing a city card. Camps do not stack with city tokens placed on the same territory.

The playing piece placed on territories to mark control. Each player has thirty-six rabbits of their chosen color. A rabbit on a territory means you control it and gain access to any resource it produces.
A ten by ten grid with coordinates labeled A through J for columns and one through ten for rows. Each space is a territory with a terrain type. Territory cards reference specific grid coordinates.
The track running around the edge of the game board. Each player's score marker moves forward as points are earned at the end of each round and after final parchment scoring.

If a territory is already occupied by another player's rabbit, you cannot place your rabbit there. Drafting a territory card for an occupied space wastes the card. Check the board before drafting to avoid this.
Cards are passed in alternating directions each round. Rounds one and three pass left, rounds two and four pass right. This ensures different players get access to different cards each round.

In a two-player game, each drafting step involves choosing two cards to keep and discarding one card face down, removing it from the game. This creates hidden information and adds a layer of uncertainty about which cards are available.

The rarest of the five resource types. Gems are never printed on any territory on the board. The only way to add gems to a fiefdom is through provision cards. Controlling gems provides an exclusive Wealth advantage.

The five resource types in Bunny Kingdom are wood, fish, carrots, mushrooms, and gems. Each distinct type in a fiefdom contributes one Wealth. Duplicate resources of the same type do not increase Wealth.
